danger_signals.visual_form: Numerical indicators in UI (negative balance display)
danger_signals.meaning: Company approaching bankruptcy; requires route optimization or new revenue streams
Passenger or cargo line not generating sufficient revenue; risk of capital loss

danger_signals.visual_form: Sparse populated areas in isometric view; few buildings in zones
danger_signals.meaning: Route not yet viable; insufficient population to sustain service

safety_signals.visual_form: Green/positive financial indicators; growing passenger count
safety_signals.meaning: Sustainable revenue generation; company financial stability

safety_signals.visual_form: Visible building density increase in isometric tile view; residential and commercial structures appearing
safety_signals.meaning: Successful delivery of building materials has triggered city development; future passenger demand increasing

safety_signals.visual_form: Highlighted areas in city grid where building materials are deposited; adjacent construction activity
safety_signals.meaning: Correct infrastructure placement; city expansion proceeding as intended

safety_signals.visual_form: Connected rail network; stations placed in populated areas
safety_signals.meaning: Stable operational framework; routes functional and integrated

faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: None—no rival companies exist in game world
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Player-constructed tracks, stations, and buildings (hotels, ski resorts); visually distinct from auto-generated city structures
Procedurally generated residential houses, shops, roads responding to material delivery; appears organically in isometric viewport

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Isometric grid with isolated buildings; large empty areas; minimal infrastructure
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: AI city growth halted; no passenger demand on routes; building materials transport only viable revenue

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Cargo visualization on rails; construction activity visible in delivery zones; new buildings appearing adjacent to depots
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Population begins migrating to developed areas; passenger demand increases in connected zones

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Dense building clusters in isometric view; extensive road networks; multiple residential and commercial structures; shops and amenities visible
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: High passenger volumes on established routes; multiple revenue opportunities (passenger service, hotel/resort income); expansion into new zones viable

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: UI displays red balance indicators; routes may appear visually degraded or disconnected if maintenance cannot be funded
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Unable to fund new infrastructure; existing services may stall; no new city development triggered

environmental_storytelling.example: Player delivers building materials to a region; within game time, houses and shops visually appear in that area. This signals player success and creates visual reward for correct strategic placement.

Near-isometric dimetric projection (similar to SimCity 2000) creates readable, layered visual presentation of city—player can intuitively understand spatial relationships, population density, and infrastructure connectivity at a glance.

Player-built stations and rails are visually prominent in the cityscape; their placement relative to buildings indicates viability of routes. Isolated infrastructure signals poor planning; integrated networks signal success.
Residential houses, commercial shops, ski resorts, and hotels each have distinct visual representation. Their presence and clustering communicate city economic function and passenger attractiveness.

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Visually represented by building density and business activity; passengers accumulate at stations in populated areas; routes show high utilization
Cargo trains move along tracks toward designated zones; construction activity spawns visually in delivery zones; buildings materialize over time
Responsive to player delivery placement; generates houses, shops, and roads orthogonally adjacent to material zones; growth intensity correlates with delivery frequency
Can fund new construction (custom hotels, ski resorts); track expansion continues; stations placed strategically; company visually expands infrastructure footprint
Infrastructure expansion halts; existing tracks and stations remain static; no new buildings or services constructed; company isolated in cityscape

notes: A-Train III (1992 SNES release via Maxis) uses near-isometric dimetric projection to communicate world state through visual density, building distribution, and infrastructure connectivity. The game contains no faction conflict; danger/safety is purely economic. World state is manifest through: (1) City density—sparse = underdeveloped, dense = viable markets; (2) Building type presence—shops/hotels = passenger demand, houses = population base; (3) UI financial indicators—red = crisis, green = success; (4) Infrastructure visibility—isolated tracks = poor routes, integrated networks = strategic success. Environmental storytelling relies heavily on responsive urban generation: player material delivery triggers visible construction, creating reward feedback loop. No combat, faction symbols, or political systems exist; world state is purely economic-infrastructural.
